topic: A person's invention is already patented by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias stared at the glossy patent document, the ink shimmering under the harsh fluorescent lights of the university office. His hands, usually buzzing with the energy of a thousand half-formed schematics, felt heavy and inert. The blueprint for the sonic resonator—his life’s work, the device that could revolutionize data transmission—was clearly marked, filed, and owned by OmniCorp. He traced the lines of the existing claims with a trembling finger, a dry, bitter taste rising in his throat. He pushed his chair back, the scrape of metal against linoleum sounding deafening in the quiet room. He didn't look at Dr. Albright, who was smiling patronizingly, offering congratulations on the 'novelty' of his concept. Elias simply gathered his worn notebook, the leather cool beneath his palm, and walked toward the exit, the weight of the unpatentable feeling settling deep in his chest, a physical pressure making every breath shallow.

[story 2]

"It’s brilliant, truly," Dr. Chen had said, tapping the paper with an amused flourish. Elias felt the blood drain from his face. He watched the legal jargon scroll across the screen—the precise wording that cemented OmniCorp’s claim. He leaned against the cold glass of the window, watching the city traffic flow below, each vehicle a symbol of unimpeded progress. Inside, everything felt static. He picked up a discarded coffee cup, turning it over and over, the cheap ceramic offering no comfort. A low hum of frustration vibrated in his jaw. He tried to articulate a rebuttal, to argue the subtle, crucial difference in his methodology, but the words caught, snagged on the invisible barrier of ownership. He sighed, a long, ragged exhalation that seemed to carry all the thwarted ambition of the last three years.

[story 3]

The prototype sat on the workbench, gleaming under the single desk lamp. It was perfect. Elias ran a fingertip over the polished casing, a gesture of pure, proprietary satisfaction. Then, he opened the digital archive. There it was: the filing date, the inventor’s name—not his. A cold wave washed over him, chilling him to the bone despite the stuffy warmth of the lab. He stood motionless, his shoulders slumping as if an invisible weight had been dropped onto his spine. He walked to the whiteboard, picked up a dry-erase marker, and began furiously sketching alternate designs, wild, desperate modifications. Each line was a frantic attempt to carve a path around the established boundary, a silent scream against the finality of the legal decree. His knuckles were white where he gripped the marker, his breathing rapid and shallow.
